# Rayleigh calibration across the whole network — time series, problematic-station diagnosis, L1 vs L2 (2026)

*Generated 2026-06-21. `validation/run_rayleigh_diag_light.py` + `analyze_rayleigh_diag.py`, on the v2
constants from the network run. All 153 CHM15k + Mini-MPL streams, 2026. The cause diagnosis uses L1
(instrument housekeeping lives only in native L1); §"L1 vs L2" compares the calibration outcome.*

## Metrics

Per stream (optimized v2):

- **clear%** = clear (fit-reaching) nights ÷ archive days — *clear-sky availability after screening*.
- **valid% (of clear)** = valid calibrations ÷ clear nights — *method yield on the nights that reach the fit*.
- **valid% (of archive)** = valid ÷ archive days = availability × yield — *overall productivity* (the sorted bars).
- **σ_SD**, **outlier%** = short-term variability and drift-aware outlier rate of the nightly constant.

Four diagnostic metrics, sampled per station, map to the candidate failure causes:

| cause | diagnostic | "bad" signature (vs healthy CHM15k median) |
|---|---|---|
| not enough clear sky | clear% | **11%** vs 33% |
| lots of FT aerosol | median window scattering ratio | **1.18** vs 1.12 |
| low laser | near-range signal strength → SNR proxy (+ `laser_life_time`) | SNR **13** vs 33 |
| electronic background | far-range noise → SNR proxy | SNR **22** vs 33 (high noise) |

A station is **problematic** if (within its type) it falls in the worst quartile of overall yield, or
the worst decile of σ_SD or outlier rate. Each is assigned the **dominant** cause (largest
standardized exceedance of the per-type threshold); secondary causes are listed too.

## Result — 59 of 153 streams problematic

cause | # streams | what it looks like
---|---|---
✅ ok | 94 | clear 33%, scat 1.12, SNR 33, σ_SD 8.9% — healthy
🟫 electronic background | 19 | low SNR from high far-range noise (clear sky fine)
🟪 not enough clear sky | 16 | clear% ~11% — Arctic/maritime, persistent cloud
🟧 low laser | 12 | low SNR from weak near-range signal; often old laser
🟥 FT aerosol | 6 | scattering ratio ≥1.18 — persistent free-tropospheric aerosol
⬜ other | 6 | flagged on σ_SD/outliers without a single dominant metric

![CHM15k network diagnosis: (top-left) overall yield sorted, coloured by cause; (top-right) clear-sky availability drives yield; (bottom-left) FT aerosol vs σ_SD; (bottom-right) SNR vs outlier rate.](data:image/png;base64,)

## What is wrong with the problematic stations

**Not enough clear sky (16).** clear% ≈ 11% (a third of healthy). High-latitude / maritime sites with
persistent cloud — **Jan Mayen, Bjørnøya, Hopen, Tórshavn** (Arctic), **Bonaire** (trade-cumulus),
**Camborne**. Not an instrument fault — too few clear nights. (But see L1-vs-L2 below: on L2 these
reach the fit far more often, though most extra nights then fail QC.)

**Low laser (12).** Low SNR from *weak near-range signal*, often with high `laser_life_time`:
**Vásárosnamény** (laser ≈ 53 900 h), **Nieuwkoop**, **Gottfrieding**. Aging/under-powered laser →
low SNR in the 2–6 km band. Action: laser service / re-collimation. **Persists on L2** (§ below).

**Electronic background (19).** Low SNR from *high far-range noise* (the fit band itself is fine):
**Friesoythe, Klippeneck, Bern**. Elevated detector/electronic background raises the noise floor.
Action: check detector baseline / dark-current. **Persists on L2.**

**FT aerosol (6).** Persistently elevated window scattering ratio (≥1.18): **Payerne (1.24), Bern**
— frequent free-tropospheric aerosol. v2's scattering gate rejects nights even when skies look clear.
Action: relaxed-scattering v2 (C8) + higher fit windows / per-site gate.

> Payerne: clear% 43% (plenty of clear nights) yet valid ≈ 1%, scat 1.24 AND SNR 20 — limited by
> **both** FT aerosol and background, not clear-sky availability.

**Mini-MPL (5):** Aléria (83%) and Lille (55%) healthy; Brest *background*, Trappes *FT-aerosol*, the
mobile unit *low-laser*.

## L1 vs L2 — same streams, same v2 method

The calibration metrics on L2 come from the same network run; the diagnosis stays on L1 (L2 carries no
instrument housekeeping). Medians over streams:

| | clear% L1→L2 | valid/clear% L1→L2 | σ_SD% L1→L2 | outlier% L1→L2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CHM15k (n=148) | **32 → 98** | 73 → 59 | 9.2 → 10.0 | 4 → 5 |
| Mini-MPL (n=5) | 92 → 92 | 61 → 67 | 8.9 → 11.6 | 10 → 8 |

![Paired per-stream L1 vs L2 (v2): clear-sky reach, yield-on-clear, σ_SD, outlier rate. Colour = L1-diagnosed cause. Points above the diagonal (panels 1–2) / below (panels 3–4) favour L2.](data:image/png;base64,)

**The dominant L1↔L2 difference is cloud *screening*, not the calibration itself.** Native **L1**
carries the full cloud-base + fog (`vertical_visibility`) flags and screens aggressively — only ~32 %
of CHM15k archive days reach the molecular fit. The **L2** reconstruction screens far more leniently —
~98 % reach the fit (Payerne: 43 % → 98 %, verified). But L2's extra nights are largely marginal:
yield-on-clear drops 73 % → 59 % (i.e. ~40 % of L2's "clear" nights fail the rel-error QC). So L2 has
higher **overall productivity** (valid/archive-day ≈ 58 % vs ≈ 23 % on L1) at **lower per-night
confidence**, with **σ_SD essentially unchanged** (9.2 → 10.0 %).

**Per cause, L1 → L2 (CHM15k median clear% / valid-on-clear% / σ_SD):**

cause | L1 | L2 | reading
---|---|---|---
ok | 33 / 74 / 8.9 | 99 / 64 / 9.8 | L2 reaches almost every day; ~36 % then fail QC; precision ~equal
not enough clear sky | 11 / 82 / 12.2 | 91 / 47 / 12.2 | **partly an L1-screening effect** — on L2 they reach the fit, but **half fail QC** (genuinely marginal) and σ_SD stays high
low laser | 34 / 43 / 10.2 | 98 / 42 / **10.0** | **persists on L2** — averaging does *not* fix a weak laser (yield-on-clear stays ~42 %)
electronic background | 30 / 35 / 10.0 | 99 / 43 / **11.7** | **persists** — L2 salvages a few more nights but σ_SD *worsens* (10.0 → 11.7)
FT aerosol | 35 / 76 / 9.7 | 99 / 61 / 9.3 | behaves like "ok"; aerosol limits both levels similarly

**Takeaway on L1 vs L2:** the two **instrument-health causes (low-laser, electronic background) appear
on both levels** — L2's averaging cannot recover a weak laser or a noisy detector, so they are genuine
hardware flags, not L1 artefacts. The **clear-sky / atmospheric** limitation is expressed differently:
L1 screens those nights out, L2 lets them reach the fit but they fail QC — either way the station stays
low-yield. If the goal is *more usable calibrations*, run on **L2** (≈2.5× the valid-night count at
comparable σ_SD); if the goal is *cleaner per-night screening*, **L1** is stricter. Note L2's lenient
screening means its QC (rel-error ≤ 15 %) is doing the real "is-this-clear" filtering, so that
threshold matters more on L2.

## Time series — full network

Every CHM15k stream's nightly lidar constant (v2, L1), sorted worst-yield first; green = robust
median, red = outliers, pink panels = problematic. 6 pages cover all 148 CHM15k; Mini-MPL separately.

![CHM15k time series page 1/6 (lowest-yield streams)](data:image/png;base64,)

## Problematic-station list

Full table (59 streams) in `figs_paper_validation/rayleigh_diag/problematic_stations.md`. Worst by yield:

site | type | valid% | clear% | σ_SD% | scat | SNR | laser(h) | cause
---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---
Tórshavn | CHM15k | 0 | 0 | – | – | 91 | 16901 | clear-sky
QUALAIR | CHM15k | 0 | 0 | – | – | 30 | 42629 | clear-sky
Friesoythe | CHM15k | 0 | 31 | – | 1.18 | 61 | 26136 | background
Bonaire | CHM15k | 0 | 4 | – | 1.15 | 76 | 16012 | background→clear-sky
Payerne | CHM15k | 1 | 43 | – | 1.24 | 20 | 8934 | FT-aerosol + background
Vásárosnamény | CHM15k | 1 | 59 | – | 1.19 | 12 | 53948 | low-laser
Jan Mayen | CHM15k | 2 | 2 | – | 1.08 | 25 | 49507 | clear-sky
Nieuwkoop | CHM15k | 2 | 34 | – | 1.13 | 13 | 9390 | low-laser

## Takeaways

- ~60 % of the CHM15k network calibrates healthily; the problematic 40 % split into four physically
  distinct, separable causes.
- **Two are not instrument faults** — *clear-sky-limited* (Arctic/maritime) and *FT-aerosol* sites are
  atmosphere-limited; they need longer accumulation or per-site gate relaxation, not repair.
- **Two are instrument-health flags worth acting on** — *low-laser* (e.g. Vásárosnamény, QUALAIR, Jan
  Mayen) and *electronic-background* stations — and **both persist on L2**, confirming they are
  hardware, not screening, issues. The far-range-noise and laser-age metrics here can drive an
  automated network health alert.
- **L1 vs L2:** the levels differ mainly in cloud screening (L1 strict ~32 %, L2 lenient ~98 % reach),
  not in the constant itself (σ_SD comparable). L2 gives ≈2.5× more valid calibrations per day but
  leans on its rel-error QC to reject the extra marginal nights.

## Reproduce
```
python validation/run_rayleigh_diag_light.py   # sampled HK + scattering per stream
python validation/analyze_rayleigh_diag.py     # summary + figures (incl. fig_l1_vs_l2) + problematic_stations.md
python validation/embed_report.py doc/reports/rayleigh_network_diagnosis_report.md   # embed figures in place
```
